topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp paper felt alien beneath my fingertips. I stared at the meticulously typed narrative, the words a stranger's voice recounting my life. Every achievement, every whispered secret, felt like a calculated performance I’d never agreed to. A cold weight settled in my chest, heavy and suffocating. I traced a line describing a childhood memory I’d only ever experienced as a vague, sun-drenched blur. My hands began to tremble, the fine tremor escalating until the edges of the page blurred. I wanted to rip it, to destroy the evidence of this invasion, but my muscles refused to obey. Silence hung in the room, thick and oppressive, broken only by the frantic drumming of my own pulse against my ribs.

[story 2]

I sat at the kitchen table, the lukewarm coffee forgotten in my hand. The biography lay open, a stark, damning testament to a life I didn't own. Reading the section detailing my supposed motivations made my stomach clench with a hollow ache. I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to physically block the text from my vision. A slow, ragged exhale escaped my lips, tasting of ash. The room seemed to shrink, the walls pressing inward. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of wood against the floor sounding deafening in the quiet apartment. I walked to the window, watching the indifferent city lights, feeling utterly disconnected, as if I were observing a film of someone else's existence.

[story 3]

The discovery hit me like a physical blow. I reread the final chapter, the carefully constructed portrait of 'me' that was entirely fabricated. A strange numbness washed over me, a profound lack of feeling where warmth used to reside. My breath hitched, shallow and uneven. I stood rigid, my spine feeling too brittle to support me. I started pacing the length of the living room, a restless, agitated energy churning beneath my skin. Each step was heavy, weighted by an unbearable sense of betrayal. I stopped abruptly, staring at my reflection in the darkened television screen—a stranger with hollow eyes—and simply let my shoulders slump, a complete surrender to the overwhelming emptiness.
